chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number
CSCE 482
Course Title
Senior Capstone Design
Section & Time
936 (TTH 08:00 am-11:00 am)
Location:
EABA 118
Credit Hours:
3

chunks.title: Instructor Details
chunks.content:
Instructor:
Jeff Huang
E-Mail:
jeffhuang@tamu.edu
Office Hours/Location: See Google Classroom

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content:
Development of system integration skills for solving real-world problems in computer science; significant team software
project that uses integration of advanced concepts across computer science specializations; projects require design,
implementation, documentation and demonstration, as well as design methodology, management process and
teamwork.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Grade of C or better in CSCE 411, and CSCE 315 or CSCE 331; senior classification.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content:
As per university requirements: 1) at least 35% of your grade will involve written or oral communications, 2) 70% of that
35% is based on individual (not group) writing or speaking, 3) at least 1250 words are individually written, 4) reviews are
provided to you to allow for revision of some number of documents or slides, 5) you have at least 5 minutes of individual
public speaking. To receive C credit for this course, you must pass the C component.

chunks.title: Use of AI tools
chunks.content:
Use of Al tools, such as ChatGPT, LLM APIs, and other Al-powered tools (e.g., Cursor, Claude Code, Codex), is permitted
in this course for students who wish to use them. To adhere to our scholarly values, students must cite any Al-generated
material that informed their work (this includes in-text citations and/or use of quotations, and in your reference list).
Using an Al tool to generate content without proper attribution qualifies as academic dishonesty. See
https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.html for information on how to
properly cite Al tools.

Note that existing Al tools come with inherent limitations. For instance, there is no assurance that the code they
generate is entirely accurate. It is your responsibility to identify and address errors in program logic through thorough
and well-documented testing. Additionally, even if the generated code is syntactically correct, it may require significant
refinement, particularly in areas such as separation of concerns, redundancies, and potential performance issues.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
This course will prepare you for engineering practice with a major design experience based on the knowledge and skills
you acquired in earlier course work. Your design will incorporate engineering standards and realistic constraints that
include most of the following considerations: economic, environmental, sustainability, manufacturability, ethical, health,
safety, and social.

To successfully complete this course, you must demonstrate the ability to**:
1. Analyze a complex computing problem and apply principles of computing and other relevant disciplines to
identify solutions.
2. Design, implement and evaluate a computing-based solution to meet a given set of computing requirements in
the context of the program's discipline.
3. Communicate effectively in a variety of professional contexts.
4. Recognize professional responsibilities and make informed judgements in computing practice based on legal and
ethical principles.
5. Collaborate effectively as a member or leader of a team engaged in activities appropriate to the program's
discipline.
6. Apply computer science theory and software implementation fundamentals to produce computing-based
solutions.

** This course is evaluated every year through the ABET (Accreditation Board for Engineering and Technology)
accreditation process, which provides assurance that a college or university program meets the quality standards of the
profession, by doing the following:
• Verifies that your educational experience meets the global standard for technical education in your profession.
• Enhances your employment opportunities-multinational corporations require graduation from an accredited
program.
• Supports your entry to a technical profession through licensure, registration, and certification—all of which
often require graduation from an ABET-accredited program as a minimum qualification.
• Establishes your eligibility for federal student loans, grants, and/or scholarships.
• Paves the way for you to work globally, because ABET accreditation is recognized worldwide through
international agreements, and other countries' national accrediting systems are based on the ABET model.

To comply with ABET, students must demonstrate their abilities in outcomes above, which will be evaluated by external
industrial affiliate members during your final presentation.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
Primary - Recommended:
Design for Electrical and Computer Engineers, by Ralph Ford and Chris Coulston (McGraw-Hill Science, 2007).
ISBN 978-0-07-338035-3

Secondary - Recommended:
• The Five Dysfunctions of a Team: A Leadership Fable. Patrick M. Lencioni. (Jossey-Bass, 2002). Available free
online at TAMU Libraries.
• Technical material from the literature, manufacturers' data sheets and user manuals (as needed).

You can acquire the course material from vendors that provide the best value and amenities.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
The final grade you will receive in the class will be based on points accumulated during the semester. Thus, both
continued progress (the process) and the quality of your product (and other deliverables) will determine your grade. Your
grade is based on the performance of your team and your individual contribution.

The grading scale is as follows:
F < 60% ≤ D < 70% ≤ C < 80% ≤ B < 90% ≤ A

This class does not use grade curving; however, we ensure that you have a clear opportunity to earn an A. For example,
89.9% is a B. Our approach emphasizes proficiency, meaning we set clear expectations regarding the work you need to
complete and the knowledge you should acquire by the end of the course to succeed and achieve an A. The grade
distribution is as follows:

1. Weekly Updates (Group,
Individual Updates)
4%
2. Peer Review
2%
3. Peer Feedback
2%
4. Ethics
2%
5. Project Management
4%
6. Project Implementation
21%
7. Project Evaluation
21%
8. Project Documentation
21%
9. Project Presentation
21%
10. Quizzes
2%

TOTAL
100%

chunks.title: Important Notes:
chunks.content:
• You will not receive a passing grade in the class if you do not have a tested working system by the due date. The
lower weighted items will significantly help you succeed in your higher-weighted items.

• Your peer feedback responses will be calculated with the instructors' assessment which can either increase /
decrease each member's overall semester score.

Instructors will base their assessment on one or more of the following: class attendance, punctuality,
participation during class, timely submission of assignments/deliverables, peer feedback results, other informal
feedback from teammates or TAs, and other actions which positively/negatively contribute to the success of the
team.

• Any team member who does not participate, after interventions have been attempted, will automatically get at
least 1 letter grade below the lowest grade of any of the team members

In order to complete the course and receive a grade for the class, students are required to do the following:

Ο All final deliverables must be submitted and approved by the teaching team, borrowed items (e.g., keys,
books, equipment) returned to their proper location or to their owner, and work areas cleaned. All team
members are required to be present at the time of the final delivery.

Ο Participate in an exit interview with industry practitioners as applicable. If the course section is selected
to be reviewed as part of the capstone project review the section's students are excused from
participating in the exit interviews.

Complete the assigned ethics related assignments.

chunks.title: 1. Weekly Updates (4%)
chunks.content:
Group Updates, Individual Updates
Each team submits a weekly report as a group and individually, using the given templates. The group reports
communicate the team's accomplishments during the previous period, which need to be measurable, and planned
accomplishments for the following week.

Team members should all be involved in taking meeting minutes, submitting team reports, and reporting during the
weekly meeting with the instructor team.

chunks.title: 2. Peer Reviews (2%)
chunks.content: You will be asked to give a review of your peers' deliverables (e.g., presentations, etc.).

chunks.title: 3. Peer Feedback (2%)
chunks.content:
You will give feedback on the performance and contribution of your other team members several times throughout the
semester. Your peer feedback responses will be calculated along with the instructors' peer feedback scores and will be
part of your individual project grade.
Prompt submission of peer feedback will be the basis of this grade. NOTE: The instructor team has the right to withhold
credit for submission of peer feedback, due to late submission, 3 days after the due date, unless you have prior approval
for late submission.

Failure to submit your peer feedback for all your fellow team members after two reminders will result in an automatic 5%
deduction of the overall grade. This policy is in place to ensure timely distribution of valuable peer feedback and cannot
be reversed even if feedback is submitted after the second reminder.

Regardless of whether you get credit or not, your final grade cannot be finalized until you complete all your peer
feedback.

chunks.title: 4. Ethics (2%)
chunks.content: The grading criteria is related to completion of the assigned ethics assignment.

chunks.title: 5. Project Management (4%)
chunks.content:
Artifacts
These represent submissions that are required during project initiation, such as skills survey, resume, and other items
required in Google Classroom.
Reports
These represent project management related reports.
Other deliverables
You are expected to choose a software engineering process model and create the corresponding artifacts. For example,
if the team chooses to use Agile Scrum, then expected artifacts are sprint & product backlogs, user stories, etc.
Depending on the project, project artifacts to be submitted include (but not limited to):
a. Data: Test data, initial seed data, etc.
b. Designs: schematics, data sheets, etc.
C. Tools: freeware software tools, compilers, etc.
d. Documentation: project related documents you are asked to submit
e. Evaluation: evaluation related documents you are asked to submit
f. Audiovisual media: close-up pictures of your system, raw footage, etc.
g. Evidence of live presentations given (e.g., program, acknowledgement letters, etc.)
h. Spare parts and supplies
i. Installed final application on one or more devices
j. Invoices / Receipts / Forms: See next section on Purchases & Reimbursements

Purchases and Reimbursements

• Accurate Forms: Please ensure that purchase order (PO) forms submitted to the department are accurate and
complete (e.g., specifications). Submitting an incomplete PO will result in the team being prevented from submitting
additional POs; all additional purchases will have to be made by team members who will be reimbursed.
• In-Stock Items: Please verify that items are in stock before submitting a PO and provide alternative vendors if an
item is likely to go out of stock (e.g., if the vendor only has a few units left). If you suspect that a critical item may
become backordered, it may be better if you purchase it directly since POs may take several days to go through.
• Reimbursements Deadline: Reimbursement requests are due at the time of final deliverables. To minimize overhead,
we will only reimburse up to five purchase orders (of your choice); this policy also encourages teams to plan their
purchases in advance. Overnight or express delivery will not be reimbursed unless approved in advance by the
instructor. Reimbursement of sales taxes is not allowed by the university; please make sure to bring tax-exempt
forms with you whenever you make local purchases (or purchases within the state).
• Equipment Return: The equipment and materials (e.g., keys, books, equipment) that you purchased or borrowed do
not belong to you, they belong to Texas A&M University, hence they need to be returned to their proper location, to
their owner, or to the instructor team.

Lastly, we expect that the facilities used by the project team have been restored to an acceptable condition as confirmed
by the instructor team.

NOTE: Grades will not be assigned until all project deliverables have been turned in or completed. All team members are
required to participate in making sure deliverables are completed and delivered.

chunks.title: 6. Project Implementation (21%)
chunks.content:
Code (together with all tests and documentation) will be submitted in the class GitHub repository,
Version Control
These represent the set-up of your version control system and regular commits to your repository.
Code/Result & Documentation
Documentation (e.g., GitHub README) and code will be critiqued on its compliance with requirements, correctness in
proper execution, and maintainability as indicated by the effort needed to understand the details of your system, by
people external to the team.

chunks.title: 7. Project Evaluation (21%)
chunks.content:
Internal Testing
You are expected to assess that the expected deliverables of the project meet implicit and explicit (e.g., functionality)
requirements. Expectations include developing automated test suites and multi-phase testing (unit / integration /
system testing). The definition of success is related to how well you prepared and implemented your evaluation plan to
meet your team's quality goals (e.g., test coverage). You are expected to submit your test results to get credit.

User Acceptance Testing Results: at least twenty participants (see the instructor if you have circumstances that warrant
having less than twenty)
This evaluates how well you prepared and executed the user acceptance test, including submission of the user
acceptance test results. Note that your grade cannot be finalized unless you complete the user acceptance test.

chunks.title: 8. Project Documentation (21%)
chunks.content:
** If you intend to publish your results, you will need to complete an IRB (ask instructor for guidance)

To fulfill the communication requirement of the class, each team is required to submit documentation with some of the
key documents described below.

Individual Proposal (for student proposed projects)
Each student will look for a problem or need of a real customer or beneficiary and develop a problem proposal for
discussion with the bigger group. Thereafter, a set of viable problems will be presented to the class as candidates for
capstone projects. Reviews from your classmates on your proposals will help define the final project idea. Likewise, you
will review your classmates' work to help refine their ideas and potentially find teammates with similar interests. Note:
Identification of a ‘real' beneficiary is required.

Problem Exploration and Discussion
Scenario 1 - projects with sponsors: You will describe your approach to the given problem statement, get your work
reviewed by your peers, and optionally give a review on your classmates' work. When the team is formed, all proposed
approaches are synthesized and become part of the final report.

Scenario 2 - projects with no sponsors, just an external beneficiary: You will iterate through potential project ideas which
have a proven need / existing beneficiary and explore your chosen project idea further.

Final Report
An overview of content in your final report is covered below. The final report will be written incrementally throughout
the semester with submission of two intermediate increments, prior to submission of the final version. Your final report
will be critiqued on its thoroughness and effectiveness in communicating key aspects of your system.

Abstract & Introduction
This is a summary of your final report that describes: your problem (with attention grabber), how you plan to
solve it, and expected outcome.

The introduction provides a brief overview of the project and helps the reader put into context the design and
implementation.

References / Related Work
To ensure that you have a strong understanding of existing relevant work you will perform a literature review of
related works to your team project. Each team member is expected to identify a minimum number of relevant
papers (details to be announced during class) for their project to be referenced in their final report. These papers
will be different for each team member (e.g., a team of four with 5 references each will have twenty papers to
reference in their final report). Related work refers to each person reading two papers and writing a reading
summary on those two papers.

Engineering Standard
Engineering standards are factors that are related to your project and may bound or constrain your project.
Ethical concerns are included here.

Requirements
The requirements part of your methodology should clearly describe what your solution seeks to achieve and
what the user experience will be. You should describe your minimum viable product and any additional features
required.

You may express requirements as narrative, feature lists, user stories, usage scenarios, models, prototypes, etc.
User stories provide a high-level description of one or more features from the perspective of an end user and
make up your product backlog. Usage scenarios are the equivalent of user stories in a non-agile process model.
Prototyping allows you to describe mockups you developed to demonstrate what your solution will look like.

Design
The design part of your methodology should clearly describe how you will implement your proposed solution,
what the user experience will be, what tools you will use, and the risks you anticipate.

Evaluation
The evaluation section describes procedures that you will use to assess that your system does what it was
designed to do.

Implementation, Results, Discussion
The implementation part of your methodology describes your system in detail including individual components,
tools used, your system hierarchy, and any other details for the audience to understand how your system works.

The results section will summarize the outcome of your project and your evaluation to show whether you
achieved your definition of success.

The discussion section includes a reflection on the results.

Future Work, Conclusion, Annexes
Future work describes new opportunities and any lingering challenges that can be undertaken in a future project.

The conclusion is a summary of your project with emphasis on the outcomes.

Annexes will contain the project plan, user manual, and additional information which supports other sections of
the final report, such as user stories which support the requirements section, design models which support the
design section, test cases which support the evaluation section, and so forth.

chunks.title: 9. Presentations (21%)
chunks.content:
Checkpoints & Demo of System Progress,
External presentations (e.g., COE Project Showcase, CSCE Showcase)
Checkpoints refer to informal presentations on specific deliverables with the purpose of assessing progress. Additionally,
your team will present to at least two other external venues, the COE Project Showcase and CSCE Showcase. There may
be other opportunities presented in class which will provide you with extra practice in communicating your work and
responding to external reviews.

Posters, Presentation Slides & In-Class Presentation; Project Review-Industry
These opportunities allow you to communicate and demonstrate your deliverables (e.g., software) to your instructors,
peers, and to industry panelists.

Demo Video & Appearance Release
The demo video is a 2-to-3-minute video which future generations of students can view as they also embark on their
capstone journey. Appearance release indicates your permission for us to share the video and other artifacts which may
have a picture / video of you.

chunks.title: 10. Quizzes (2%)
chunks.content:
The quizzes will include knowledge that you should have gained from your past computer science courses, and lets you
know the topics that external reviewers may ask about or bring up during external presentations. External reviewers
expect that you have the ability to communicate the skills or knowledge you acquired in your undergraduate studies.

This category also includes participation exercises given in class. Any item in this category is expected to be done in class,
otherwise taking this outside of class may constitute an honors violation, as determined by the honors council.

chunks.title: Class Attendance
chunks.content:
Importance of Attendance: Not attending weekly class meetings harms the other members of your group and makes it
much more difficult for the instructor to assess your contributions to the group effort. Due to the undue burden your
frequent absences have on the rest of the team, more than five unexcused absences are an automatic failure.

The instructors will evaluate your attendance through frequent quizzes, meetings, participation in the discussions,
participation in assignments, and/or contributions to the team. Upon instructors' discretion, arriving to class late (> 5
minutes late), leaving early (> 5 minutes before class end), and/or not participating (e.g., not taking a quiz, not being
present for a status meeting, not submitting in class work, and/or doing work not related to the class) can be considered
an unexcused absence, depending on the circumstances. Oversleeping, a term paper due, an exam to cram for, etc., are
not valid reasons. The instructor reserves the right to determine what constitutes a "valid reason" on a case-by-case
basis.

Absence / lateness during days in which teams may need to work in a remote location to accomplish certain assignments
(e.g., presenting your work outside of class, interviewing experts, meeting with mentors, accessing technologies outside
of the classroom, etc.) may count toward the 5 unexcused absences (at the discretion of the instructor), unless the
absence / lateness are approved by the instructor in advance. Students are expected to meet instructor's requirements
for check in, activity reporting requirements, and/or all in-class assignments are submitted on time.

You are responsible for monitoring / tracking your unexcused absences.

Students experiencing personal injury or illness that is too severe for the student to attend class qualify for an excused
absence (see Student Rule 7, Section 7.2.2) assuming that documentation and notification guidelines are followed.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Late work policy is defined as submitting a deliverable after the established deadline. Work submitted by a student as
makeup work for an excused absence, according to Student Rule 7 is not considered late work and is exempted from the
late work policy. Late work approvals must clearly link to an excused absence request per Student Rule 7.

If you feel that you have a valid reason for submitting late work, even if it is not related to Student Rule 7, submit a
request to excuse the late submission in advance of the deadline (3 business days in advance is required, except in an
emergency). We will carefully review your request and will either approve or disapprove the request (with fairness and
equity in mind) if the request has been submitted in a timely manner. Without a request, late submissions will incur a
penalty of 2% per hour. Submissions that are more than two days late will receive zero credit.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Below is an overview of the overall schedule, subject to change. Refer to Google Classroom for the most up-to-date
schedule.

chunks.title: Week(s) Topics / Activities
chunks.content:
| Week(s) | Topics / Activities |
| :------ | :------------------ |
| 1-4 | Introduction to Senior Capstone Design:
• Identify and formulate an engineering problem, how to function effectively in teams, how your
project fits in the context of other work, how to communicate the motivation, need, and ethical
considerations for the system, needs & requirements identification and how to define success in
the project, project management.
• Assessment: Iterative progress on project management & other deliverables |
| 5-7 | Project Design, Implementation, & Evaluation
• Deriving a design based on needs and requirements identification
• How to create an evaluation plan according to the team's definition of success.
• Skill development in preparation for implementation
• Assessment: Iterative progress on project management, documentation, implementation,
evaluation (e.g., testing), & other deliverables |
| 8-11 | Project Implementation & Evaluation
• Communicate the user interface design and design choices
• Continue implementation & evaluation, including testing & reviews
• Conduct user acceptance tests to evaluate the success of their system.
• Assessment: Iterative progress on project management, documentation, implementation,
evaluation (e.g., testing), & other deliverables |
| 12-14 | Project Implementation, Evaluation, & Presentations
• Complete user acceptance test to evaluate the success of their system.
• Determine results and their implications.
• Assessment: Iterative progress on project management, documentation, implementation,
evaluation (e.g., testing), in-class & external demonstrations, & other deliverables |
| 15-16 | Finalize project implementation, presentations and deliverable submission
• Capstone project review with industry (if selected) or exit interviews with industry
• Final Assessment: Iterative progress on project management, documentation, implementation,
evaluation (e.g., testing), in-class & external demonstrations, & other deliverables |

chunks.title: Course Copyright
chunks.content:
The materials used within this course are copyrighted and include, but are not limited to, the syllabi, quizzes, exams,
homework, online handouts, course videos, audio, and visual recordings of classes, etc. Because these materials are
copyrighted, you do not have the right to copy or distribute these materials, unless permission is expressly granted.

chunks.title: Academic Freedom
chunks.content:
Academic freedom is a cornerstone of the University. Academic freedom in its teaching aspect is fundamental for the
protection of the rights of the teacher in teaching and of the student to freedom in learning.

1. Each faculty member is entitled to full freedom in the classroom discussing the subject which the faculty
member teaches.

2. Texas A&M will not penalize or discipline members of the faculty because of their exercise of academic freedom.

Along with this freedom comes responsibility. It is the responsibility of faculty members to ensure that topics
discussed are related to the classroom subject. Students should be free to take reasoned exception to the data or
views offered in any course of study and to reserve judgment about matters of opinion, but they are responsible
for learning the content of any course of study for which they are enrolled.

3. It is not the proper role of the university or any outside agency to attempt to shield individuals from ideas and
opinions they find unwelcome, disagreeable, or even deeply offensive.

4. Engaging with new ideas and perspectives helps students grow intellectually and is beneficial to the educational
process.

chunks.title: Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
The university views class attendance and participation as an individual student responsibility. Students are expected to
attend class and to complete all assignments.

Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about excused absences, including definitions, and related
documentation and timelines.

chunks.title: Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content:
Students will be excused from attending class on the day of a graded activity or when attendance contributes to a
student's grade, for the reasons stated in Student Rule 7, or other reason deemed appropriate by the instructor.

Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about makeup work, including definitions, and related
documentation and timelines.

Absences related to Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972 may necessitate a period of more than 30 days for
make-up work, and the timeframe for make-up work should be agreed upon by the student and instructor" (Student
Rule 7, Section 7.4.1).

“The instructor is under no obligation to provide an opportunity for the student to make up work missed because of an
unexcused absence" (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.2).

Students who request an excused absence are expected to uphold the Aggie Honor Code and Student Conduct Code.
(See Student Rule 24.)

chunks.title: Academic Integrity Statement and Policy
chunks.content:
"An Aggie does not lie, cheat or steal, or tolerate those who do."

“Texas A&M University students are responsible for authenticating all work submitted to an instructor. If asked, students
must be able to produce proof that the item submitted is indeed the work of that student. Students must keep
appropriate records at all times. The inability to authenticate one's work, should the instructor request it, may be
sufficient grounds to initiate an academic misconduct case" (Section 20.1.2.3, Student Rule 20).

You can learn more about the Aggie Honor System Office Rules and Procedures, academic integrity, and your rights and
responsibilities at aggiehonor.tamu.edu.

Writing: Use of Al tools, including ChatGPT, is permitted in this course for students who wish to use them. To adhere to
our scholarly values, students must cite any Al-generated material that informed their work (this includes in-text citations
and/or use of quotations, and in your reference list). Using an Al tool to generate content without proper attribution
qualifies as academic dishonesty. See
https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.html for information how to
properly cite Al tools. (adapted from University of Massachusetts Amherst,
https://cte.tamu.edu/getmedia/1d5e4ef6-97f1-4065-987f-3c9dfecbb7bd/TAMU-CTE_GenAI-SyllabusStatementConsidera
tions.pdf)

Computer Code. In principle you may submit Al-generated code, or code that is based on or derived from Al-generated
code, as long as this use is properly documented in the comments: you need to include the prompt and the significant
parts of the response. Al tools may help you avoid syntax errors, but there is no guarantee that the generated code is
correct. It is your responsibility to identify errors in program logic through comprehensive, documented testing.
Moreover, generated code, even if syntactically correct, may have significant scope for improvement, in particular
regarding separation of concerns and avoiding repetitions. The submission itself must meet our standards of attribution
and validation. (from Boris Steipe (2023) “Syllabus Resources”. The Sentient Syllabus Project http://sentientsyllabus.org.)

You can learn more about the Aggie Honor System Office Rules and Procedures, academic integrity, and your rights and
responsibilities at aggiehonor.tamu.edu.

chunks.title: Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) Policy
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University is committed to providing equitable access to learning opportunities for all students. If you
experience barriers to your education due to a disability or think you may have a disability, please contact the Disability
Resources office on your campus (resources listed below) Disabilities may include, but are not limited to attentional,
learning, mental health, sensory, physical, or chronic health conditions. All students are encouraged to discuss their
disability-related needs with Disability Resources and their instructors as soon as possible.

Disability Resources is located in the Student Services Building or at (979) 845-1637 or visit disability.tamu.edu.

chunks.title: Title IX and Statement on Limits to Confidentiality
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University is committed to fostering a learning environment that is safe and productive for all. University
policies and federal and state laws prohibit gender-based discrimination and sexual harassment, including sexual assault,
sexual exploitation, domestic violence, dating violence, and stalking.

With the exception of some medical and mental health providers, all university employees (including full and part-time
faculty, staff, paid graduate assistants, student workers, etc.) are Mandatory Reporters and must report to the Title IX
Office if the employee experiences, observes, or becomes aware of an incident that meets the following conditions (see
University Rule 08.01.01.M1):

• The incident is reasonably believed to be discrimination or harassment.
• The incident is alleged to have been committed by or against a person who, at the time of the incident, was (1) a
student enrolled at the University or (2) an employee of the University.

Mandatory Reporters must file a report regardless of how the information comes to their attention – including but not
limited to face-to-face conversations, a written class assignment or paper, class discussion, email, text, or social media
post. Although Mandatory Reporters must file a report, in most instances, a person who is subjected to the alleged
conduct will be able to control how the report is handled, including whether or not to pursue a formal investigation. The
University's goal is to make sure you are aware of the range of options available to you and to ensure access to the
resources you need.

Students wishing to discuss concerns related to mental and/or physical health in a confidential setting are encouraged to
make an appointment with University Health Services or download the TELUS Health Student Support app for 24/7
access to professional counseling in multiple languages. Walk-in services for urgent, non-emergency needs are available
during normal business hours at University Health Services locations; call 979.458.4584 for details.

Students can learn more about filing a report, accessing supportive resources, and navigating the Title IX investigation
and resolution process on the University's Title IX webpage.

chunks.title: Statement on Mental Health and Wellness
chunks.content:
Statement on Mental Health and Wellness
Texas A&M University recognizes that mental health and wellness are critical factors influencing a student's academic
success and overall wellbeing. Students are encouraged to engage in healthy self-care practices by utilizing the resources
and services available through University Health Services.

The TELUS Health Student Support app provides access to professional counseling in multiple languages anytime,
anywhere by phone or chat, and the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline offers 24-hour emergency support at 988
or 988lifeline.orgLinks to an external site..

Students needing a listening ear can contact University Health Services (979.458.4584). 24-hour emergency help is also
available through the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (988) or at 988lifeline.orgLinks to an external site.
